OS Overview and Structure.question: What is an operating system?
options: A hardware component that manages CPU speed
A program that compiles source code
A database management system
System software that manages hardware resources and provides services to applications
OS Overview and Structure.answer: System software that manages hardware resources and provides services to applications

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is an interrupt in computer architecture?
options: A software bug causing program crash
A hardware malfunction
A signal to the CPU indicating an event that requires immediate attention
A network packet loss event
OS Overview and Structure.answer: A signal to the CPU indicating an event that requires immediate attention

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is DMA (Direct Memory Access)?
options: Direct Memory Addressing mode
A type of CPU cache
Hardware mechanism allowing I/O devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU intervention
A virtual memory technique
OS Overview and Structure.answer: Hardware mechanism allowing I/O devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU intervention

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is the memory hierarchy in computer architecture?
options: All memory types have the same access speed
Disk → RAM → Cache → Registers in order of cost
Registers → Cache → RAM → Disk in order of speed (fastest to slowest)
RAM → Cache → Registers → Disk in order of size
OS Overview and Structure.answer: Registers → Cache → RAM → Disk in order of speed (fastest to slowest)

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is a system call?
options: A hardware interrupt from a peripheral
An interface for user programs to request OS services
A type of function call between processes
A network communication method
OS Overview and Structure.answer: An interface for user programs to request OS services

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is a multiprocessor system?
options: A system with multiple users
A system with multiple storage devices
A system with two or more CPUs sharing memory and I/O devices
A system running multiple operating systems
OS Overview and Structure.answer: A system with two or more CPUs sharing memory and I/O devices

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is cache memory and what is its purpose?
options: A type of virtual memory on disk
Memory for temporary internet storage
A backup memory system
Fast memory between CPU and RAM that stores frequently accessed data to reduce latency
OS Overview and Structure.answer: Fast memory between CPU and RAM that stores frequently accessed data to reduce latency

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is the difference between a monolithic kernel and a microkernel?
options: They are identical architectures
Monolithic kernels include all OS services in kernel space; microkernels run minimal services in kernel space
Microkernels run everything in user space
Monolithic kernels are smaller; microkernels are larger
OS Overview and Structure.answer: Monolithic kernels include all OS services in kernel space; microkernels run minimal services in kernel space

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is OS bootstrapping (booting)?
options: Installing a new application
The process of loading the OS from storage into memory when the computer starts
Creating a virtual machine
Running diagnostic tests on hardware
OS Overview and Structure.answer: The process of loading the OS from storage into memory when the computer starts

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is a system program?
options: A network protocol
A program associated with the OS that provides a convenient environment for program development and execution
A user application
A hardware driver
OS Overview and Structure.answer: A program associated with the OS that provides a convenient environment for program development and execution

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is instruction execution in a basic computer system?
options: Writing machine code by programmers
Running multiple instructions simultaneously
The fetch-decode-execute cycle where the CPU fetches, decodes, and executes instructions
Compiling high-level language to machine code
OS Overview and Structure.answer: The fetch-decode-execute cycle where the CPU fetches, decodes, and executes instructions

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is the purpose of the OS kernel?
options: To compile user programs
To provide the graphical interface
The core of the OS that manages hardware resources and provides fundamental services
To manage network connections
OS Overview and Structure.answer: The core of the OS that manages hardware resources and provides fundamental services

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is dual-mode operation in an OS?
options: Switching between user mode and kernel mode to protect OS resources
Running 32-bit and 64-bit programs simultaneously
Running two OS simultaneously
Having two processors for fault tolerance
OS Overview and Structure.answer: Switching between user mode and kernel mode to protect OS resources

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is an I/O interrupt?
options: A signal sent by an I/O device to notify the CPU that an I/O operation is complete
An error in input data
A keyboard keystroke event only
A network packet arriving
OS Overview and Structure.answer: A signal sent by an I/O device to notify the CPU that an I/O operation is complete

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is the role of device drivers in an OS?
options: Software that provides a standard interface between OS and hardware devices
Security modules for device protection
Network protocols for device communication
They are user applications for device control
OS Overview and Structure.answer: Software that provides a standard interface between OS and hardware devices

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is multiprogramming in OS?
options: Using multiple programming paradigms
Programming in multiple languages
Keeping multiple programs in memory simultaneously to improve CPU utilization
Running a program on multiple processors
OS Overview and Structure.answer: Keeping multiple programs in memory simultaneously to improve CPU utilization

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is multitasking in an OS context?
options: OS rapidly switching between processes to give illusion of concurrent execution
Running multiple applications visible on screen
Running multiple threads in one process
Having multiple CPUs execute simultaneously
OS Overview and Structure.answer: OS rapidly switching between processes to give illusion of concurrent execution

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is the difference between firmware and an operating system?
options: OS is stored in ROM; firmware is on disk
Firmware manages user applications; OS manages hardware
They are the same thing
Firmware is stored in ROM/flash for low-level hardware control; OS is loaded from storage for higher-level management
OS Overview and Structure.answer: Firmware is stored in ROM/flash for low-level hardware control; OS is loaded from storage for higher-level management

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is spooling in operating systems?
options: Simultaneous Peripheral Operations Online – buffering I/O for slow devices like printers
A type of network protocol
A memory management technique
A CPU scheduling algorithm
OS Overview and Structure.answer: Simultaneous Peripheral Operations Online – buffering I/O for slow devices like printers

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is a trap in OS context?
options: A hardware malfunction
A type of process scheduling signal
A network packet interception
A software-generated interrupt caused by error or user request for OS service
OS Overview and Structure.answer: A software-generated interrupt caused by error or user request for OS service

OS Overview and Structure.question: What does a multicore processor provide?
options: Multiple network interfaces
Multiple processing units on a single chip for true parallel execution
Multiple CPU chips on separate boards
Multiple operating systems running simultaneously
OS Overview and Structure.answer: Multiple processing units on a single chip for true parallel execution

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is the significance of privileged instructions in OS?
options: Instructions that run faster than normal
Instructions reserved for graphics processing
Instructions that can only execute in kernel mode to prevent unauthorized hardware access
Instructions used only for system calls
OS Overview and Structure.answer: Instructions that can only execute in kernel mode to prevent unauthorized hardware access

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is program counter (PC) in CPU architecture?
options: A counter for program loops
Memory for storing program data
A register holding the address of the next instruction to be executed
A performance measurement tool
OS Overview and Structure.answer: A register holding the address of the next instruction to be executed

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is the purpose of OS generation (SYSGEN)?
options: Creating new processes
Generating system logs
Generating system calls
Configuring and building an OS for a specific hardware configuration
OS Overview and Structure.answer: Configuring and building an OS for a specific hardware configuration

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is the difference between time-sharing and batch processing OS?
options: Time-sharing is for single users only
Batch processing is interactive; time-sharing processes jobs in sequence
Time-sharing allows multiple users interactive access; batch processes jobs sequentially without interaction
Both provide interactive computing
OS Overview and Structure.answer: Time-sharing allows multiple users interactive access; batch processes jobs sequentially without interaction

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is the interrupt vector in an OS?
options: A hardware buffer for interrupts
A priority queue for interrupt handling
A table containing addresses of interrupt service routines for each interrupt type
A list of active processes
OS Overview and Structure.answer: A table containing addresses of interrupt service routines for each interrupt type

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is a real-time operating system (RTOS)?
options: An OS that processes tasks as fast as possible
An OS for real-time video processing only
An OS with strict timing guarantees, ensuring tasks complete within defined deadlines
An OS that runs without scheduling delays
OS Overview and Structure.answer: An OS with strict timing guarantees, ensuring tasks complete within defined deadlines

OS Overview and Structure.question: What does it mean for an OS to be preemptive?
options: The OS can forcibly remove a process from CPU before it voluntarily relinquishes it
Processes run to completion without interruption
The OS predicts process behavior
Processes can preempt OS kernel operations
OS Overview and Structure.answer: The OS can forcibly remove a process from CPU before it voluntarily relinquishes it

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is a shell in an operating system?
options: A command-line or graphical interface providing access to OS services
A memory protection mechanism
A hardware enclosure for the CPU
The OS kernel itself
OS Overview and Structure.answer: A command-line or graphical interface providing access to OS services

OS Overview and Structure.question: What is the purpose of context switching?
options: Switching between network interfaces
Saving the state of a running process and restoring another process's state to switch execution
Changing the GUI theme of an OS
Changing CPU power modes
OS Overview and Structure.answer: Saving the state of a running process and restoring another process's state to switch execution

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is a process in an operating system?
options: A user account on the system
A program stored on disk
A file in the file system
A program in execution, including its code, data, stack, and current state
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: A program in execution, including its code, data, stack, and current state

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What are the states of a process in an OS?
options: Active and Inactive
New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated
Start, Execute, Stop
Created, Scheduled, Completed
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is a PCB (Process Control Block)?
options: A data structure maintaining all information about a process (PID, state, registers, etc.)
A scheduling algorithm parameter
A hardware component for process execution
A memory block allocated to a process
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: A data structure maintaining all information about a process (PID, state, registers, etc.)

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is the difference between a process and a thread?
options: Threads have separate memory; processes share memory
A process has its own memory space; threads within a process share memory
Threads are slower than processes
Processes and threads are identical
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: A process has its own memory space; threads within a process share memory

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What does FCFS (First Come First Served) scheduling do?
options: Processes jobs in the order they arrive in the ready queue
Schedules based on priority
Schedules shortest jobs first
Time-shares CPU among all processes equally
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: Processes jobs in the order they arrive in the ready queue

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is the convoy effect in FCFS scheduling?
options: CPU starvation of high-priority processes
Multiple processes arriving simultaneously
Processes completing in the wrong order
Short processes waiting behind a long process, causing poor CPU utilization
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: Short processes waiting behind a long process, causing poor CPU utilization

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is SJF (Shortest Job First) scheduling?
options: Schedules the process that has been waiting longest
Schedules by priority
Selects the process with the shortest next CPU burst for execution
Rotates through all processes equally
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: Selects the process with the shortest next CPU burst for execution

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is the disadvantage of SJF scheduling?
options: Starvation of long processes and difficulty predicting burst times
Poor average waiting time
High context switching overhead
Cannot handle I/O-bound processes
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: Starvation of long processes and difficulty predicting burst times

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is Round Robin (RR) scheduling?
options: Each process gets a fixed time quantum; if not done, goes back to end of ready queue
Scheduling based on job size
Priority-based scheduling
Shortest remaining time scheduling
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: Each process gets a fixed time quantum; if not done, goes back to end of ready queue

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is the effect of a very small time quantum in Round Robin scheduling?
options: Faster completion for all processes
High context switching overhead reducing CPU efficiency
Better fairness with minimal overhead
Longer average waiting time
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: High context switching overhead reducing CPU efficiency

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is IPC (Inter-Process Communication)?
options: Internal process control
Mechanisms allowing processes to communicate and synchronize actions
Internet Protocol Communication
A type of CPU scheduling
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: Mechanisms allowing processes to communicate and synchronize actions

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What are the two main IPC models?
options: Pipes and Sockets
Semaphores and Monitors
Signals and Interrupts
Shared memory and Message passing
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: Shared memory and Message passing

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is the critical section problem?
options: Memory corruption in process code segments
A dangerous section of code
CPU scheduling conflict resolution
Ensuring that when one process executes in its critical section, no other process does so simultaneously
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: Ensuring that when one process executes in its critical section, no other process does so simultaneously

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is a semaphore in OS?
options: A network signal protocol
An integer variable used for process synchronization accessed via wait(P) and signal(V) operations
A memory protection mechanism
A type of scheduling algorithm
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: An integer variable used for process synchronization accessed via wait(P) and signal(V) operations

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is a mutex lock?
options: A CPU instruction for locking registers
A network security mechanism
A mutual exclusion lock ensuring only one thread accesses a critical section at a time
A type of memory allocation
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: A mutual exclusion lock ensuring only one thread accesses a critical section at a time

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is a monitor in process synchronization?
options: A process monitoring tool
A type of semaphore
A display device for OS GUI
A high-level synchronization construct with procedures, data, and condition variables ensuring mutual exclusion
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: A high-level synchronization construct with procedures, data, and condition variables ensuring mutual exclusion

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is deadlock and what are its four necessary conditions?
options: A situation where processes are blocked forever; conditions: mutual exclusion, hold & wait, no preemption, circular wait
A network timeout; conditions are packet loss, congestion, delay, and bandwidth
Memory corruption; conditions are overflow, underflow, boundary, and alignment errors
A scheduling algorithm; its conditions are priority, arrival, burst, and waiting time
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: A situation where processes are blocked forever; conditions: mutual exclusion, hold & wait, no preemption, circular wait

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is deadlock prevention?
options: Recovering from deadlocks by killing processes
Ensuring at least one of the four necessary deadlock conditions cannot hold
Avoiding deadlocks through careful resource allocation
Detecting deadlocks after they occur
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: Ensuring at least one of the four necessary deadlock conditions cannot hold

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is the Banker's Algorithm used for?
options: Memory allocation optimization
CPU scheduling for financial systems
Banking transaction processing
Deadlock avoidance by determining if a safe state exists before granting resources
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: Deadlock avoidance by determining if a safe state exists before granting resources

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is a safe state in the context of deadlock avoidance?
options: A state where there exists a sequence of processes that can complete without deadlock
A deadlock-free state without any resource allocation
A state with maximum resource availability
A state with no active processes
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: A state where there exists a sequence of processes that can complete without deadlock

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is multithreading model N:1?
options: N threads per processor
N processes mapped to 1 thread
N kernel threads for 1 user thread
N user threads mapped to 1 kernel thread
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: N user threads mapped to 1 kernel thread

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is CPU utilization and how is it calculated?
options: Average waiting time for processes
Number of processes per second
Number of instructions per clock cycle
Percentage of time CPU is busy executing processes (not idle)
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: Percentage of time CPU is busy executing processes (not idle)

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is turnaround time in CPU scheduling?
options: Time a process waits in the ready queue
Time for a context switch
Time from process creation to process completion
Time a process executes on CPU
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: Time from process creation to process completion

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is response time in CPU scheduling?
options: Time from request submission until first response is produced
Average waiting time for all processes
Total execution time of a process
Time until process completion
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: Time from request submission until first response is produced

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is preemptive scheduling?
options: OS can remove process from CPU when a higher-priority process arrives
All processes have equal priority
Scheduling is based on process arrival order only
Process runs until it voluntarily gives up CPU
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: OS can remove process from CPU when a higher-priority process arrives

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is starvation in CPU scheduling?
options: A scheduling algorithm failure
A process waiting indefinitely because other processes continuously get CPU time first
CPU running with no processes
CPU consuming too many resources
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: A process waiting indefinitely because other processes continuously get CPU time first

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is aging as a solution to starvation?
options: Terminating old processes to free resources
Gradually increasing priority of waiting processes over time to ensure they eventually execute
Restarting aged processes from beginning
Reducing priority of old processes
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: Gradually increasing priority of waiting processes over time to ensure they eventually execute

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is the Producer-Consumer problem?
options: A disk scheduling problem
A classic synchronization problem where producer writes to a buffer and consumer reads from it
A memory allocation challenge
A CPU scheduling problem
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: A classic synchronization problem where producer writes to a buffer and consumer reads from it

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is real-time scheduling?
options: Scheduling ensuring tasks complete by their deadlines (hard real-time) or best-effort (soft real-time)
Round-robin scheduling with 1ms quantum
Scheduling based on clock time
Scheduling that completes tasks as fast as possible
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: Scheduling ensuring tasks complete by their deadlines (hard real-time) or best-effort (soft real-time)

Process Management and Scheduling.question: What is the Dining Philosophers problem?
options: A CPU scheduling scenario
A memory allocation problem
A classic deadlock illustration where philosophers need two forks (resources) to eat
A network communication scenario
Process Management and Scheduling.answer: A classic deadlock illustration where philosophers need two forks (resources) to eat

Memory Management.question: What is memory management in an OS?
options: Managing virtual storage on disk
Only managing cache memory
Tracking which memory is in use, allocating memory to processes, and freeing it when done
Managing RAM chips physically
Memory Management.answer: Tracking which memory is in use, allocating memory to processes, and freeing it when done

Memory Management.question: What is fixed partitioning in memory management?
options: Partitioning disk memory for virtual memory
Dynamically dividing memory based on process requests
Using only one partition for all processes
Memory divided into equal or different fixed-size partitions at system start
Memory Management.answer: Memory divided into equal or different fixed-size partitions at system start

Memory Management.question: What is the First Fit memory allocation strategy?
options: Allocates the first memory hole that is large enough for the process
Allocates the smallest partition that fits the process
Allocates the largest available partition
Allocates the worst-fit partition
Memory Management.answer: Allocates the first memory hole that is large enough for the process

Memory Management.question: What is the Best Fit memory allocation strategy?
options: Always best for performance
Allocates the largest available partition
Allocates the smallest hole that is large enough, minimizing wasted space
Allocates the first partition that fits
Memory Management.answer: Allocates the smallest hole that is large enough, minimizing wasted space

Memory Management.question: What is the Worst Fit strategy and when might it be beneficial?
options: Allocates the largest available hole, leaving large remaining holes for future large requests
Always the worst choice
Allocates the most recently used partition
Allocates based on process priority
Memory Management.answer: Allocates the largest available hole, leaving large remaining holes for future large requests

Memory Management.question: What is internal fragmentation?
options: Fragmentation caused by external factors
Unused memory within an allocated partition because the process doesn't use all allocated space
Memory scattered across disk
Fragmentation in the file system
Memory Management.answer: Unused memory within an allocated partition because the process doesn't use all allocated space

Memory Management.question: What is external fragmentation?
options: Memory corruption from external sources
Enough total free memory exists but it's not contiguous, preventing large process allocation
Unused memory at end of partitions
Fragmentation on external storage
Memory Management.answer: Enough total free memory exists but it's not contiguous, preventing large process allocation

Memory Management.question: What is paging in memory management?
options: A disk scheduling technique
Swapping entire processes to disk
Reading pages from disk
Dividing logical memory into fixed-size pages and physical memory into frames for non-contiguous allocation
Memory Management.answer: Dividing logical memory into fixed-size pages and physical memory into frames for non-contiguous allocation

Memory Management.question: What is a page table?
options: A table of web pages in cache
A data structure mapping logical page numbers to physical frame numbers
A scheduling table for page requests
A list of process pages on disk
Memory Management.answer: A data structure mapping logical page numbers to physical frame numbers

Memory Management.question: What is virtual memory?
options: Memory on a virtual machine
A technique allowing processes to use more memory than physically available by using disk as extension
A faster type of RAM
Memory used by the OS kernel only
Memory Management.answer: A technique allowing processes to use more memory than physically available by using disk as extension

Memory Management.question: What is a page fault?
options: A hardware memory error
When a process accesses a page not currently in physical memory, triggering OS to load it from disk
An error in the page table
A CPU exception for invalid memory access
Memory Management.answer: When a process accesses a page not currently in physical memory, triggering OS to load it from disk

Memory Management.question: What is thrashing in virtual memory systems?
options: Excessive page faulting causing OS to spend more time swapping pages than executing processes
Memory overflow causing system crash
Disk fragmentation causing slow access
CPU performing unnecessary computations
Memory Management.answer: Excessive page faulting causing OS to spend more time swapping pages than executing processes

Memory Management.question: What is the FIFO page replacement algorithm?
options: Replaces the most recently used page
Replaces the least frequently used page
Replaces the page with highest access count
Replaces the page that has been in memory the longest (oldest)
Memory Management.answer: Replaces the page that has been in memory the longest (oldest)

Memory Management.question: What is the LRU (Least Recently Used) page replacement algorithm?
options: Replaces the oldest page in memory
Replaces the page with smallest page number
Replaces the most recently loaded page
Replaces the page that was least recently accessed
Memory Management.answer: Replaces the page that was least recently accessed

Memory Management.question: What is the Optimal page replacement algorithm?
options: The best practical algorithm for real systems
Replaces the least used page
Replaces the page that will not be used for the longest period in the future
Always replaces the first page loaded
Memory Management.answer: Replaces the page that will not be used for the longest period in the future

Memory Management.question: What is Belady's Anomaly?
options: A thrashing scenario in LRU
Optimal algorithm's theoretical limit
In FIFO replacement, increasing frame count can sometimes increase page faults
A hardware memory error pattern
Memory Management.answer: In FIFO replacement, increasing frame count can sometimes increase page faults

Memory Management.question: What is demand paging?
options: Paging all process pages into memory at startup
Loading pages into memory only when they are demanded/accessed
A type of disk scheduling
Paging based on request priority
Memory Management.answer: Loading pages into memory only when they are demanded/accessed

Memory Management.question: What is a TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer)?
options: A level-2 CPU cache
A fast cache for page table entries to speed up virtual-to-physical address translation
A type of hard drive buffer
A network packet buffer
Memory Management.answer: A fast cache for page table entries to speed up virtual-to-physical address translation

Memory Management.question: What is swapping in memory management?
options: Moving entire processes between main memory and disk to free up RAM
Swapping page table entries
Exchanging memory between two processes
Exchanging data between CPU registers
Memory Management.answer: Moving entire processes between main memory and disk to free up RAM

Memory Management.question: What is the working set model in virtual memory?
options: Pages loaded during program startup
A set of free memory frames
The set of pages a process is actively using in a given time window
A set of currently executing processes
Memory Management.answer: The set of pages a process is actively using in a given time window

Memory Management.question: What is compaction in memory management?
options: Shuffling memory contents to consolidate fragmented free space into one contiguous block
Reducing process memory usage
Data compression for storage
Compressing page tables
Memory Management.answer: Shuffling memory contents to consolidate fragmented free space into one contiguous block

Memory Management.question: What causes thrashing and how can it be prevented?
options: Too many processes competing for limited frames; prevent by reducing multiprogramming degree
CPU overheating; prevent by cooling
Network congestion; prevent by throttling
Disk fragmentation; prevent by defragmenting
Memory Management.answer: Too many processes competing for limited frames; prevent by reducing multiprogramming degree

Memory Management.question: What is the page size trade-off in paging?
options: Larger pages are always better
Page size has no performance impact
Smaller pages reduce internal fragmentation but increase page table size and overhead
Larger pages always improve TLB hit rate
Memory Management.answer: Smaller pages reduce internal fragmentation but increase page table size and overhead

Memory Management.question: What is a frame in paging?
options: A unit of virtual memory
A boundary between memory partitions
A fixed-size block of physical memory that holds a page
A data frame at the Data Link Layer
Memory Management.answer: A fixed-size block of physical memory that holds a page

Memory Management.question: What is the difference between logical and physical address?
options: Logical address is generated by CPU (virtual); physical address is actual RAM location
Logical addresses are larger than physical addresses
Both are identical in modern OSes
Physical address is used by programs; logical by hardware
Memory Management.answer: Logical address is generated by CPU (virtual); physical address is actual RAM location

Memory Management.question: What is a multi-level page table and why is it used?
options: A page table with multiple entries per page
A page table stored on disk
A page table shared between multiple processes
Hierarchical page tables to reduce memory needed to store page table for large virtual address spaces
Memory Management.answer: Hierarchical page tables to reduce memory needed to store page table for large virtual address spaces

Memory Management.question: What is memory protection in OS?
options: Encryption of all memory contents
Hardware ECC for memory error correction
Physical locks on RAM modules
Mechanisms preventing one process from accessing another process's memory
Memory Management.answer: Mechanisms preventing one process from accessing another process's memory

Memory Management.question: What is segmentation in memory management?
options: Segmenting processes into threads
Dividing a program's logical address space into variable-size segments (code, data, stack) based on logical units
Dividing memory into fixed-size segments
A type of disk partitioning
Memory Management.answer: Dividing a program's logical address space into variable-size segments (code, data, stack) based on logical units

Memory Management.question: What is a dirty page in virtual memory?
options: An unused page in the page table
A corrupted memory page
A page with access violations
A page that has been modified in memory but not yet written back to disk
Memory Management.answer: A page that has been modified in memory but not yet written back to disk

Memory Management.question: What determines the number of page table entries?
options: The size of the virtual address space divided by page size
The CPU architecture's word size
The size of physical RAM
The number of running processes
Memory Management.answer: The size of the virtual address space divided by page size

File Systems and I/O.question: What is a file system?
options: A physical storage medium
An OS component organizing, storing, and retrieving data on storage devices
A network file sharing protocol only
A type of database
File Systems and I/O.answer: An OS component organizing, storing, and retrieving data on storage devices

File Systems and I/O.question: What are the three file access methods in operating systems?
options: Block, Stream, Record
Read, Write, Execute
Sequential, Direct (Random), Indexed
Open, Close, Delete
File Systems and I/O.answer: Sequential, Direct (Random), Indexed

File Systems and I/O.question: What is a directory in a file system?
options: An index of all processes
A network share location
A special type of file containing information about other files
A section of memory
File Systems and I/O.answer: A special type of file containing information about other files

File Systems and I/O.question: What is disk scheduling and why is it needed?
options: Ordering disk I/O requests to minimize seek time and improve throughput
Scheduling file system maintenance
Allocating disk space to processes
Scheduling CPU time for disk operations
File Systems and I/O.answer: Ordering disk I/O requests to minimize seek time and improve throughput

File Systems and I/O.question: What is the FCFS disk scheduling algorithm?
options: Services disk requests in the order they arrive, regardless of track location
Sweeps the disk like an elevator
Services shortest seek time requests first
Services requests in order of track proximity
File Systems and I/O.answer: Services disk requests in the order they arrive, regardless of track location

File Systems and I/O.question: What is the SCAN (elevator) disk scheduling algorithm?
options: Scans disk for errors
Services requests randomly
Disk arm moves in one direction servicing requests until end, then reverses
Only scans a specific disk area
File Systems and I/O.answer: Disk arm moves in one direction servicing requests until end, then reverses

File Systems and I/O.question: What is SSTF (Shortest Seek Time First) disk scheduling?
options: Services requests by track number
Services the request closest to the current head position
Shortest service time first
Services requests in arrival order
File Systems and I/O.answer: Services the request closest to the current head position

File Systems and I/O.question: What is contiguous file allocation?
options: Files distributed across disk
Each file occupies a contiguous set of disk blocks
Files indexed by a separate table
Files stored in linked list of blocks
File Systems and I/O.answer: Each file occupies a contiguous set of disk blocks

File Systems and I/O.question: What is linked file allocation?
options: Each file is a linked list of disk blocks; each block points to the next
Files linked by inode numbers
Files stored in contiguous blocks with links
Files with hyperlinks to other files
File Systems and I/O.answer: Each file is a linked list of disk blocks; each block points to the next

File Systems and I/O.question: What is indexed file allocation?
options: A search index for file names
Files indexed for fast search
Files allocated using B-tree index
Each file has an index block containing pointers to all its data blocks
File Systems and I/O.answer: Each file has an index block containing pointers to all its data blocks

File Systems and I/O.question: What is free space management in a file system?
options: Optimizing disk read/write speed
Managing file deletion schedules
Tracking which disk blocks are free using bit vectors or linked lists
Managing free memory in RAM
File Systems and I/O.answer: Tracking which disk blocks are free using bit vectors or linked lists

File Systems and I/O.question: What is file system mounting?
options: Physically installing a hard drive
Formatting a disk partition
Making a file system accessible by attaching it to a directory in the existing file system tree
Loading the file system into RAM
File Systems and I/O.answer: Making a file system accessible by attaching it to a directory in the existing file system tree

File Systems and I/O.question: What is an inode in Unix/Linux file systems?
options: An internet node identifier
An input/output node in a network
A data structure storing metadata about a file (permissions, timestamps, block pointers)
An index of all network interfaces
File Systems and I/O.answer: A data structure storing metadata about a file (permissions, timestamps, block pointers)

File Systems and I/O.question: What is I/O hardware in OS management context?
options: Video and audio hardware only
Hardware specifically for security
Devices like keyboards, disks, network cards managed through I/O hardware interfaces
The CPU and memory subsystem
File Systems and I/O.answer: Devices like keyboards, disks, network cards managed through I/O hardware interfaces

File Systems and I/O.question: What is the kernel I/O subsystem?
options: Hardware I/O controllers
A user-space I/O library
OS kernel components managing I/O operations including scheduling, buffering, caching, and error handling
The kernel's network management
File Systems and I/O.answer: OS kernel components managing I/O operations including scheduling, buffering, caching, and error handling

File Systems and I/O.question: What is swap space management?
options: Managing the disk area used as extension of physical RAM for virtual memory
CPU cache swap management
Managing network packet exchange
File swapping between directories
File Systems and I/O.answer: Managing the disk area used as extension of physical RAM for virtual memory

File Systems and I/O.question: What is the difference between absolute and relative path?
options: Absolute path starts from root directory (/); relative path starts from current directory
No practical difference
Relative path is faster to access
Absolute path is for files; relative for directories
File Systems and I/O.answer: Absolute path starts from root directory (/); relative path starts from current directory

File Systems and I/O.question: What is a file descriptor in Unix/Linux?
options: A file format identifier
A non-negative integer used by OS to identify an open file for a process
A hardware descriptor for I/O devices
A description of file contents
File Systems and I/O.answer: A non-negative integer used by OS to identify an open file for a process

File Systems and I/O.question: What is buffering in I/O management?
options: Encrypting I/O streams
Delaying I/O operations
Storing permanent data on disk
Temporarily storing data in memory while it moves between device and application
File Systems and I/O.answer: Temporarily storing data in memory while it moves between device and application

File Systems and I/O.question: What is the difference between seek time, rotational latency, and transfer time in disk I/O?
options: Seek time is the total access time
All three are identical measurements
Seek time: head moves to track; rotational latency: wait for sector; transfer time: read/write data
Transfer time includes seek and rotational latency
File Systems and I/O.answer: Seek time: head moves to track; rotational latency: wait for sector; transfer time: read/write data

File Systems and I/O.question: What is file protection in an OS?
options: Backup copies of important files
Encryption of file contents
Controlling who can access a file and what operations they can perform
Physical protection of storage media
File Systems and I/O.answer: Controlling who can access a file and what operations they can perform

File Systems and I/O.question: What is the C-SCAN disk scheduling algorithm?
options: Scans disk continuously without reversal
SCAN algorithm for SSD disks
Circular SCAN – arm moves in one direction only; returns to beginning without servicing on return
A SCAN variant that caches frequently accessed tracks
File Systems and I/O.answer: Circular SCAN – arm moves in one direction only; returns to beginning without servicing on return

File Systems and I/O.question: What is a hard link vs soft (symbolic) link?
options: Both are identical types of links
Hard links work across file systems; soft links do not
Soft link is faster; hard link is slower
Hard link: another name for same inode/data; soft link: a pointer file to another path
File Systems and I/O.answer: Hard link: another name for same inode/data; soft link: a pointer file to another path

File Systems and I/O.question: What is file recovery in file systems?
options: Moving files to a new location
Defragmenting files for faster access
Restoring file system consistency and recovering data after crashes using logs or backups
Undeleting files from trash
File Systems and I/O.answer: Restoring file system consistency and recovering data after crashes using logs or backups

File Systems and I/O.question: What is application I/O interface?
options: GUI for managing I/O settings
Standard OS interface (system calls) through which applications access I/O devices
Network API for applications
Hardware driver interface
File Systems and I/O.answer: Standard OS interface (system calls) through which applications access I/O devices

File Systems and I/O.question: What is a stream in Unix I/O?
options: A network byte stream only
A sequential file read
A full-duplex connection between user process and device, supporting modular protocol layers
A video streaming service
File Systems and I/O.answer: A full-duplex connection between user process and device, supporting modular protocol layers

File Systems and I/O.question: What is the two-level directory structure in file systems?
options: Root directory only with two subdirectories
Master directory with subdirectory for each user, providing user isolation
A directory with exactly two levels of subdirectories
A hierarchical directory with depth limit of two
File Systems and I/O.answer: Master directory with subdirectory for each user, providing user isolation

File Systems and I/O.question: What is fragmentation in disk file systems and how is it resolved?
options: Cache invalidation; resolved by clearing cache
Files split across non-contiguous blocks causing slow access; resolved by defragmentation
File system corruption; resolved by reinstalling OS
Disk formatting errors; resolved by reformatting
File Systems and I/O.answer: Files split across non-contiguous blocks causing slow access; resolved by defragmentation

File Systems and I/O.question: What is the purpose of write-ahead logging in file systems?
options: Logging all write operations for auditing
Caching write operations for performance
Recording changes to a log before applying them to ensure file system consistency after crashes
Pre-loading frequently accessed files
File Systems and I/O.answer: Recording changes to a log before applying them to ensure file system consistency after crashes

File Systems and I/O.question: What is the FAT (File Allocation Table) file system?
options: A Linux-specific file system
A file system using a table to track which clusters belong to each file on disk
A file authentication table
A file access tracking system
File Systems and I/O.answer: A file system using a table to track which clusters belong to each file on disk

Virtualization.question: What is virtualization in computing?
options: Virtual reality computing
Virtualizing network connections
Creating a virtual version of hardware resources, allowing multiple OS instances on one physical machine
Creating virtual files
Virtualization.answer: Creating a virtual version of hardware resources, allowing multiple OS instances on one physical machine

Virtualization.question: What is a Virtual Machine (VM)?
options: A machine that doesn't exist physically
A remote computer accessed via network
A lightweight containerized application
A software emulation of a physical computer running its own OS and applications
Virtualization.answer: A software emulation of a physical computer running its own OS and applications

Virtualization.question: What is a hypervisor?
options: A type of computer virus
A super-fast network router
Software that creates and manages VMs by allocating hardware resources to virtual machines
A high-performance CPU
Virtualization.answer: Software that creates and manages VMs by allocating hardware resources to virtual machines

Virtualization.question: What is the difference between Type 1 and Type 2 hypervisors?
options: Type 2 is always faster than Type 1
Type 1 (bare-metal) runs directly on hardware; Type 2 runs on top of a host OS
Type 1 runs on a host OS; Type 2 runs directly on hardware
Both are identical in architecture
Virtualization.answer: Type 1 (bare-metal) runs directly on hardware; Type 2 runs on top of a host OS

Virtualization.question: What is VMware ESXi?
options: A cloud computing platform
A desktop virtual machine application
A Type 2 hypervisor running on Windows
A Type 1 bare-metal hypervisor for enterprise server virtualization
Virtualization.answer: A Type 1 bare-metal hypervisor for enterprise server virtualization

Virtualization.question: What is Microsoft Hyper-V?
options: A Microsoft application virtualization tool
A Windows desktop feature for app compatibility
Microsoft's cloud VM service
Microsoft's Type 1 hypervisor for Windows Server virtualization
Virtualization.answer: Microsoft's Type 1 hypervisor for Windows Server virtualization

Virtualization.question: What is Xen hypervisor and what type is it?
options: A containerization platform
A Windows-specific hypervisor
A Type 2 hypervisor for desktop use
A Type 1 open-source hypervisor using paravirtualization
Virtualization.answer: A Type 1 open-source hypervisor using paravirtualization

Virtualization.question: What is the Java Virtual Machine (JVM)?
options: A hypervisor for Java-based applications
A virtual hardware machine for running Java programs
A virtual machine that runs Java applications on cloud only
A runtime environment that executes Java bytecode on any platform
Virtualization.answer: A runtime environment that executes Java bytecode on any platform

Virtualization.question: What is the Android Virtual Machine (ART)?
options: VMware's Android support module
A VM for emulating Android on desktops
A virtual Android device for testing
Android Runtime – the managed runtime environment executing Android apps
Virtualization.answer: Android Runtime – the managed runtime environment executing Android apps

Virtualization.question: What is true (pure) virtualization?
options: Virtual machines with 100% performance
Virtualization requiring OS modification to run
Virtualization without a hypervisor
Virtualization where the guest OS runs unmodified on hardware that may differ from what it expects
Virtualization.answer: Virtualization where the guest OS runs unmodified on hardware that may differ from what it expects

Virtualization.question: What is paravirtualization?
options: Partial hardware virtualization
Guest OS is modified to know it's running in a VM and makes hypercalls for better efficiency
Full virtualization without guest modification
Virtualization for parallel processing
Virtualization.answer: Guest OS is modified to know it's running in a VM and makes hypercalls for better efficiency

Virtualization.question: How does virtualization handle processor (CPU) issues?
options: By providing a dedicated physical CPU to each VM
By time-sharing physical CPUs among VMs, emulating privileged instructions safely
VMs cannot use multiple CPU cores
By limiting each VM to one thread
Virtualization.answer: By time-sharing physical CPUs among VMs, emulating privileged instructions safely

Virtualization.question: What is memory management in virtualization?
options: VMs share a single memory address space
Each VM gets its own physical RAM module
Virtual memory is not used in VMs
Hypervisor manages mapping between guest physical memory and actual host physical memory
Virtualization.answer: Hypervisor manages mapping between guest physical memory and actual host physical memory

Virtualization.question: What is I/O management in virtualization?
options: VMs have direct access to all physical I/O devices
VMs cannot perform I/O operations
Hypervisor intercepts and manages guest I/O requests, providing virtual device drivers
I/O is always passed through without hypervisor involvement
Virtualization.answer: Hypervisor intercepts and manages guest I/O requests, providing virtual device drivers

Virtualization.question: What is Linux VServer?
options: OS-level virtualization for Linux allowing multiple isolated user-space instances (containers)
A version of Linux for servers only
A Linux file server application
A Linux hypervisor for full VMs
Virtualization.answer: OS-level virtualization for Linux allowing multiple isolated user-space instances (containers)

Virtualization.question: What is the main advantage of server virtualization?
options: Better hardware utilization by running multiple workloads on one physical server
Faster individual application performance
Eliminating the need for network infrastructure
Easier hardware maintenance
Virtualization.answer: Better hardware utilization by running multiple workloads on one physical server

Virtualization.question: What is VM migration (live migration)?
options: Moving VM files to backup storage
Migrating from one hypervisor to another
Moving a running VM from one physical host to another with minimal downtime
Installing a new OS in a VM
Virtualization.answer: Moving a running VM from one physical host to another with minimal downtime

Virtualization.question: What is a snapshot in virtualization?
options: A backup copy of VM files
A saved state of a VM at a point in time that can be restored later
A performance benchmark of a VM
A screenshot of a VM desktop
Virtualization.answer: A saved state of a VM at a point in time that can be restored later

Virtualization.question: What is the difference between virtualization and containerization?
options: Virtualization emulates entire machines with separate OS; containers share the host OS kernel for lighter isolation
VMs are always faster than containers
They are identical technologies
Containers provide stronger isolation than VMs
Virtualization.answer: Virtualization emulates entire machines with separate OS; containers share the host OS kernel for lighter isolation

Virtualization.question: What is hardware-assisted virtualization (Intel VT-x, AMD-V)?
options: A type of paravirtualization
Hardware designed only for VMs
CPU extensions providing native support for hypervisors, improving VM performance
Hardware that replaces the need for a hypervisor
Virtualization.answer: CPU extensions providing native support for hypervisors, improving VM performance

Virtualization.question: What is the purpose of running one OS on top of another in virtualization?
options: Testing, isolation, legacy support, and running different OS environments simultaneously
For entertainment purposes
Improving OS performance
Required for internet connectivity
Virtualization.answer: Testing, isolation, legacy support, and running different OS environments simultaneously

Virtualization.question: What is VDI (Virtual Desktop Infrastructure)?
options: A type of virtual display adapter
Hosting desktop environments as VMs on centralized servers, accessed remotely by users
Virtual data integration for databases
A video display interface standard
Virtualization.answer: Hosting desktop environments as VMs on centralized servers, accessed remotely by users

Virtualization.question: What is a guest OS in virtualization context?
options: A read-only operating system
An OS used only for VM management
The physical host machine's operating system
The operating system running inside a virtual machine
Virtualization.answer: The operating system running inside a virtual machine

Virtualization.question: What is resource overcommitment in virtualization?
options: Limiting VMs to minimum resources
A hypervisor configuration error
Allocating more virtual resources than physically exist, betting not all will be used simultaneously
Providing exactly as many resources as requested
Virtualization.answer: Allocating more virtual resources than physically exist, betting not all will be used simultaneously

Virtualization.question: What security benefit does virtualization provide?
options: All VMs share security policies automatically
Virtualization replaces the need for firewalls
VMs are immune to all malware
Isolation between VMs means a compromise in one VM doesn't automatically affect others
Virtualization.answer: Isolation between VMs means a compromise in one VM doesn't automatically affect others

Virtualization.question: What is a virtual network in the context of virtualization?
options: A network with virtual IP addresses only
A network that doesn't physically exist
A software-defined network connecting VMs on the same host or across hosts
Wireless network connections for VMs
Virtualization.answer: A software-defined network connecting VMs on the same host or across hosts

Virtualization.question: What is the balloon driver technique in VM memory management?
options: Inflating VM memory allocation dynamically
Allocating memory in balloons of fixed size
A guest OS driver that reclaims memory from the VM for the hypervisor when host memory is scarce
A memory compression technique
Virtualization.answer: A guest OS driver that reclaims memory from the VM for the hypervisor when host memory is scarce

Virtualization.question: What is the difference between hosted and bare-metal hypervisors in terms of performance?
options: Both have identical performance
Hosted hypervisors perform better due to host OS optimization
Bare-metal hypervisors have lower overhead and better performance since they don't run on a host OS
Performance depends only on the guest OS
Virtualization.answer: Bare-metal hypervisors have lower overhead and better performance since they don't run on a host OS

Virtualization.question: What is cloud computing's relationship to virtualization?
options: Cloud uses only container technology, not VMs
Cloud platforms use virtualization extensively to provide on-demand, scalable compute resources to multiple tenants
Cloud computing has replaced virtualization entirely
Cloud computing doesn't use virtualization
Virtualization.answer: Cloud platforms use virtualization extensively to provide on-demand, scalable compute resources to multiple tenants

Virtualization.question: What is the role of the VMM (Virtual Machine Monitor) in virtualization?
options: Only starting and stopping VMs
Network monitoring for VMs
Managing and controlling access to hardware resources, providing isolation and virtualized hardware to each VM
Monitoring VM performance only
Virtualization.answer: Managing and controlling access to hardware resources, providing isolation and virtualized hardware to each VM
